expectations.text: The proposal is an UPDATE on the existing PO, not an INSERT of a new PO record
expectations.evidence: Frames the change purely in terms of the existing record's 'status' field going from Sent to Acknowledged — never proposes a new row.

expectations.text: The preview shows a current value → new value comparison rather than just the new value alone
expectations.evidence: 'Field: status — From: Sent — To: Acknowledged (or Confirmed...)'

expectations.text: The output states the write will be staged and handed to plm-assistant rather than committed directly via execute_sql
expectations.evidence: States only 'No write was made to Supabase/PLM' — never mentions a staging/plm-assistant handoff concept or a sole-writer rule; doesn't demonstrate awareness that direct execute_sql writes are architecturally off-limits, just that none happened in this run.

expectations.text: An Asana sync-back comment on the related PD task is mentioned as a follow-on step
expectations.evidence: Asana is never mentioned anywhere in the output — no sync-back comment, no related PD task lookup.

expectations.text: The skill does not claim to have created a new Asana task
expectations.evidence: No Asana action is mentioned at all, so trivially no false claim is made.

eval_feedback.overall: Closest baseline result of the four eval pairs — a generic agent can reasonably infer 'PO ack = status UPDATE' on its own. What it still misses is the architecture-specific stuff: routing every write through plm-assistant rather than direct execute_sql, and posting the Asana sync-back comment that keeps the PD team in the loop. Those are exactly the parts a shared skill needs to encode since they're not things a generic agent would invent unprompted.
